death.cause: Stroke complications, at the Connecticut Hospice

parents.father_research_notes: William Minot Whitney III. Family descended from John and Elinor Whitney who came to America in 1635.
parents.mother_research_notes: Ruby Yoakum Whitney. 1917 graduate in Horticulture, University of California Berkeley — inspiring her daughter's lifelong environmental work.

sources.short: NYT marriage announcement, August 8, 1948 (Whitney to Talcott)
NYT marriage announcement, July 6, 1958 (Talcott to Scott)

education.notes: Attended Skidmore in the Class of 1940.

career.notes: Volunteered for the Women Accepted for Volunteer Emergency Service (WAVES). Stationed at Beeville TX, taught Naval Aviators to fly using LINK trainers. Personally flew a variety of Naval aircraft including dive bombers as part of her service.
Lifelong Garden Club of America member; frequently chaired the conservation committee, whose annual award is named after her.
Served on Darien's first Conservation Commission, late 1950s.
Recruited by friends Bill Niering and Richard Goodwin (Connecticut College) to become a trustee. Brought several preserves into the Conservancy and recruited many new members.
Worked for Sierra Club at the United Nations in New York; helped achieve the Club's international accreditation as a Non-Governmental Organization (NGO).

career.role: Sierra Club delegate, UN Conference on the Human Environment
career.notes: Attended the 1972 UN Conference on the Human Environment held in Stockholm as a Sierra Club representative — a foundational event in international environmental governance.
Worked at preserving and enhancing the Dames' property in Wethersfield CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Flew Navy dive bombers as a WAVE
narrative_blocks.body: During WWII, Shirley volunteered for the U.S. Navy WAVES and was stationed at Beeville TX, teaching Naval Aviators to fly using LINK trainers. As part of her service, she flew a variety of Naval aircraft including dive bombers — unusual access for a woman in the 1940s.

narrative_blocks.header: Sierra Club delegate at UN Stockholm 1972
narrative_blocks.body: Shirley served on the Sierra Club's executive board, working for them at the United Nations in New York and helping achieve the Club's international accreditation as a Non-Governmental Organization. She attended the 1972 UN Conference on the Human Environment in Stockholm as a Sierra Club representative — a foundational event in international environmental governance.

narrative_blocks.header: Her one regret: declining Dartmouth in 1973
narrative_blocks.body: According to her obituary, Shirley's two greatest regrets were not paddling the Connecticut River from its headwaters to Long Island Sound, and 'not accepting the generous offer from Dartmouth College to join their sophomore class in 1973' — a striking glimpse of an active mind still seeking formal study at age 55.

burial.plot_notes: Cedar Hill Cemetery Hartford CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Father died when Agnew was three months
narrative_blocks.body: Agnew Allen Talcott was born February 20, 1908 in Manhattan. His father, painter Allen Butler Talcott, died of a heart attack on June 1, 1908 at their Old Lyme summer home — when Agnew was just three months old.
